is m5_workbegin / m5_workend markers switch faster than Checkpoint restore + switch
?

Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". - mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.

Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. content.text:
**For a single run:** m5_workbegin/m5_workend is typically faster - no checkpoint I/O, just an in-process CPU switch (drain + hand off state).

**For N runs (parameter sweeps):** Checkpoint wins decisively.

| Method | Cost |
|--------|------|
| m5_workbegin | N * (fast-sim-to-ROI + switch overhead) |
| Checkpoint | 1 * checkpoint-create + N * restore-time |

The checkpoint restore is just memory load + CPU state init, so it amortizes well. The switch overhead with KVM is non-trivial - KVM drain, vCPU teardown, state migration to timing CPU.

**Practical rule:** use m5_workbegin for one-off exploratory runs; use checkpoint for any experiment with >1 config sweep.

message.content: what do you mean by "parameter sweeps"?

content.text:
Running the same workload multiple times with different gem5 config parameters - e.g., varying cache size, CPU clock, branch predictor type, DRAM bandwidth.

Each sweep iteration needs to reach the same ROI. With checkpoints, you restore once per iteration instead of re-simulating the warmup phase each time.

message.content: don't undersatnd  , restore checkpoint do we have to warmup?

content.text:
Good point - restoring a checkpoint skips warmup entirely. The checkpoint captures full system state (memory, CPU registers, cache contents) at the ROI start, so detailed simulation begins immediately.

m5_workbegin still has to fast-simulate from boot to the marker each run. That's the cost that checkpoint eliminates on repeat runs.

message.content: does m5_workbegin approach need to warmup?

content.text:
No - if you're running fast-forward (atomic/KVM) to the marker, there's no meaningful microarchitectural warmup either, because atomic mode doesn't model caches/branch predictors accurately anyway.

Both approaches have the same warmup problem: the detailed simulator starts with cold caches. The difference is just **time to reach the ROI**, not warmup quality.

If you want proper warmup, you need to simulate some instructions before the ROI in detailed mode - that's the same cost regardless of which method you use to get there.

lastPrompt: does m5_workbegin approach need to warmup?
